What is programming
programming is a process of writing a instruction which computer can understand. Instruction means code which we write to perform some tasks.

2.Compiler
It is a software which converts human readable codes into machine readable code at once, ex : c, c++, java.

3. Interpreter
It is a software which converts human readable codes into machine readable code at a line by line code. ex : python, JavaScript.

##Programming languages are classified into two types: 
low level languages and high level languages

** Low Level Language : It directly interact with hardware, It is fast and difficult. ex: Assembly language and machine code.

** High level language : It is easy to read and write, It needs a translator to translate a human readable codes into machine codes, To translate we use either compiler or interpreter, It is Slower as compare to level language. ex: c++, java.

## What is Python?
: Python is high level interpreter programming language, it was developed by Guido van Rossum in 1991, It is used to web development , data science , data analytic, AIML, automation etc.
It is known for its easy syntax which is easy to learn.

## What is Syntax?
: In programming syntax means set of rules that define the structure and format of a programming language and features of python(easy to learn, interpretred language, cross platform, use library support, open source , object oriented programming, functional programming, scalable and fast)
Note: To create a python file the extension should be .py. ex: demo.py.
Note : Print() = It is a inbuilt function which is used to display some output in our terminal .

## Comments:
Comments are notes written in code that aree ignored by compiler or interpreter when the program runs, Comments are for human to explain the code or to temporarily disable a part of code , To create comments in python there are two ways :
1.	Single line comments : use # symbol at the starting of the line to create a single line comments.

2. Multi line comments : Technically python does not have a built in multi line comments syntax but we can create by using triple quote (''') ("""). 

##Variables:
Variables are the containers which are used to store some value or data in a programming language , Rules to create variables : 

1. Name should not have any space 
2. Name should not start a number 
3.No special characters are allow except underscore(_) 
4. Keywords can not be used to create a variable 

Data Types are the category which specify which type of value a variable can store.
##Number Types :
There are three types of number types 
a. Int: It stores whole positive or negative number. ex= a =10, b=-20
b. float type : it stores floating point or decimal positive or negative numbers. ex: 1..23
c. Complex Types : It is the combination of real number and imaginary part.
-This is used for scientific calculations.
eg: a+xj, 5+aj

##Boolean Type:-
It represents only two value that is True and False
It is used for logical operations.

##Sequence Types:-
It stores ordered collection of items in a single variable.
There are three types of sequence variables :

a. String : Sequence of characters which is written inside single or doble quotes.
eg: str = "Hello", 'Hi'

b. Lists: It is a collection of multiple values in a single variable.
It is mmutable in nature
It is denoted by square Bracket [].
eg: name = ['sumit', 'Golu']

c. Touple: It is a collection of multiple values in a single variable.
It is immutable in nature. It can't be changed once created.
It is denoted by curly braces {}
eg: marks = {10,20,30}


##Set Types:-
It is a collection of unordered unique value.
It is denoted by curly braces
eg: item = {1,1,2,3,4,4,5,6}
output will be unique 

## Mapping Types
It has one data type:

a. Dictionary (dict) :- It is a collection of unordered mutable key value pairs

We can store any type of value in a single dictionary.
eg:- object = {
name : 'ABAC',
marks : 60,
age : 20
}
It is denoted by curly braces and each key value pair is separated by comma(,).

##None Types:-
None means Nothing 
It represents the absence of a value.
eg: a = None

Note:- type() function is used to check the data type of a variable or object.


##Operators 
Operators are the symbols or keywords used to perform 
Operations on variables and value or operands.
eg : a+b
a-b
a/b
a*b

here a and b are operands and '+,-,/,*' are the operators

1. Arithmetic Operators:- used for mathematical operations with numeric values.
+:Addition
-:Subtraction
/:Division
*:Multiplication
%:Modulus(Remainder)
**:Power or exponent
//:Floor division(Round Value)

2. Assignment Operators:- Used to assign value to variables.
=  : Assigns value to variable
+= : Adds and Assigns value to the left operand
-= : Subtract and assign value to the left operand
*= : Multiply and assign value to the left operand
/= : Divide and assign value to the left operand
//=: Flloor divide and assign value to the left operand
%= : Modulus and assign value to the left operand
**=: Powers and assign value to the left operand

3. Comparison Operator(Relational):- Used to Compare value and it returns only two values either true or false.

== (Equals to) -> returns True if both the operandds are same
!= (Not Equals to) -> Returns True if both the operands are not same
>(Greater than) -> Returns True if left side operand is greater than right operand
<(Less than) -> Returns True if right operand is greater than left operands
>=(Greater than equal to) :-> It returns true if left operand is greater or same as right operand
<=(Less than equal to) -> It returns true if left opernad is less or same as right operand


4.Logical Operator: Used for logical AND, OR, NOT operation
AND -> Returs true if both the operands are true, else false
OR -> Returns true if any the operand are true , if both are false then returns false
NOT -> Returns the Oppostive Value


5. Membership Operator:- Used to check if a value or variable is found im a sequence or not.
in -> Returns True if value found in the sequence.
not in -> Returns true if value not found in the sequence 

6. Identity Operator: Checks Whether Two objects share the same memory location.
is -> Returns True if Operand refer to same object.
is not -> Returns True if operand refer to different object

##Conditional Statements
It is a statement that controls the flow of a program by executing certain code blocks only if specific condition are satisfied.
With these statements we can make decisions in our codes that's why it is also known as decision making statements.

##Types of Conditional Statements:

1.If statements : If only executes the block if the condition is true
Syntax: if condition:
  instructions
if the condition is false nothing happens
2.If-Else Statements: It executes if block only when the condition is true otherwise the else block will executes. When we have two sets of instructions but one condition and we want to execute only one block of code in that situation we can use if else statements.
Syntax: if condition:
  instructions
  else:
  Instructions

3.elif Statements : It is used to check multiple conditions in a sequence , the first condition that's true will get executed if none are true then else block will execute.
It is used for several cases handling.
Syntax: if condition:
  instructions
  elif condition:
  instructions
  elif condition:
  instructions
  else:
  Instructions

##Loops: A loop is used to repeat a block of code multiple times until a specific condition is false.
Loops are used to automate the tasks, to avoid the duplicacy and making code efficient.
Types of Loops:

For Loop: It is used to iterate over a sequence or range.
It executes it's body or block of code once for each iteration in the sequence or range.
Syntax: for condition:
  instructions
for i in range()
  Instructions

2. While Loop: It executes it's body as long as the condition is true best to use when we don't know the stopping point.
Syntax: While condition:
  Instructions

3. Nested Loop: It means a loop inside another loop, it is useful when we have multidimensional data like matrices.
ex: for i in range(1,5)
  for j in range(1,5)
  print(i,j)

Note:
Break-> It is used to exit the loop, Loop body must be indented
Continue: It is used to keep the rest code and jump to next code

##String: A string is a sequence of characters used to represent text.
Strings are enclosed in single quote, double quote or triple quote.

##Property of String:
- Strings are index base collection where each and every character can be accessed by their individual index which starts from Zero.
- Strings can’t be changed after creation because strings are immutable in nature. Any operations that modify a string returns a new string
- String Supports Slicing -> s[1:6]
- Strings are case Sensitive, where a and A are different characters.
- Strings supports concatenation and repetition

Note: Use 'len' method to find length.
S[5:] - It returns a substring up to last characters.
s[:5] - It reruns a substring from starting index (0th index).
eg: s= "Hello"
print(s[-2]) //l

##String inbuilt methods
Lower():- It returns a string in a lower case. eg: print(s.lower())
Upper():- It returns a string in upper case. eg: print(s.upper())
Capitalize():- It returns a string with first letter capital. eg: print(s.capitalize())
title() :- It Converts the first character of each word to uppercase. eg: print(s.title())
Split() :- It splits a string into lists buy a specified separator. eg: print(s.split(" "))
join() :- It joins elements of iterable with string. eg: print(" " .join(s))
replace() :- It replace a substring with a new string. It accepts two arguments. Search Value and new Value. eg: print(s.replace('a','aa'))
strip() :- It removes the starting and ending whitespaces. eg: print(s.strip())
lstrip() :- It removes the starting whitespaces only. eg: print(s.lstrip())
rstrip() :- It removes the ending whitespaces only. eg: print(s.rstrip())
find() :- It returns the first occurrence index of a substring. eg: print(s.find('o'))
rfind() :- It returns the last occurrence index of a substring . eg: print(s.rfind())
index() :- It returns the index number of a substring if found, if not found then returns a ValueError. eg: print(s.index('o'))
isalpha() :- It checks if all the characters are alphabetic , it returns the value in Boolean.eg: print(s.isalpha())
isdigit() :- It checks all the characters are digits or not , it returns value in Boolean . eg: print(s.isdigit())
isalnum() :- It checks if all characters are alpha numeric or not, it returns value in Boolean. eg: print(s.isalnum())
isdecimal() :- It checks if the value is decimal or not, it returns value in Boolean. eg: print(s.isdecimal())
count() :- It counts total occurrence of a substring. eg: print(s.count('l'))
startswith() :- It checks if the string starts with a substring , it returns value in Boolean. eg: print(s.startswith('h'))
endswith() :- It checks if the string end with a substring, it returns value in Boolean. eg: print(s.endswith())

##functions in python :-
a function is a reusable block of code that perform a specific task. Functions help organize code reduce repetition and improve readability.
Once we declare a function, we can execute it multiple times whenever we need by calling the function. To create a function we have to use a keyword "def" followed by the function name , (), ; and a code block.

Functions are two types : Predefined function and user defined function.
Predefined function means, a function which is already declared by the python developer to perform a specific task. To use a pre defined function we just need to call the function.
eg:
User defined function means a function which is created or declared by the user to perform a specific task. eg: add, sum
Functions can return a value using a return statement/keyword.
Syntax : def functionName():
  #instructions
e.g:- def sum():
  print (10+20)

user defined functions are two types :
1. Functions without parameter
2. Functions with parameter: 
eg: def sub(a,b)
  return a-b

  sub(10,20), sub(200,100)
- we can pass the default value a parameter in a function to handle error if argument is missing .
- We can switch the position of parameters while passing value as an argument but we have to mention the parameter name to assigning the value.

##Dictionary
a dictionary is a mutable and unordered collection of key value pairs.
Each key must be unique.
dictionary can store any type of values.
Dictionary are denoted by {}

#Characteristic of Dictionary:
1. Key value pair - Data is stored as pairs by using single colon. e.g: key:value.
2. Unordered - Items are not stored in a sequence and the order may change.
3. Mutable - Dictionary can be modified after created.
4. No Duplicate key - No key should be duplicate, duplicate key will override the previous value.
-> To create a dictionary either we use curly braces {} or we can use dict() constructor.
e.g: example = dict(key = value, key= value)
-Also we can create an empty dictionary.
e.g: my_dict = {}
Operations on Dictionary:-
1. To add a new key :-
dictionaryName['keyName'] = Value
2. To edit a key:-
dictionaryName['keyName'] = Value
3. To delete an key :-
del dictionaryName['keyname']
4. To iterate through a dictionary :-
(a). for key,value in dictionaryName.items():
  print(key,value)
(b). for key in dictionaryName:
  print(key)


OOP(Object oriented programming)

It is a programming paradigm that is based on the concept of "objects" which can contains data and code that manipulates the data.

Key concept of OOPs:-
1.Class:- A class is a blueprint for creating object that encapsulate data and methods.
-Class can be created by "class" keyword
-A class Can have constructor (__int__) and define their own behavior via methods.
Syntax: class className:
  constructor(self, parameters):
  self.variable = parameter
  def methodName(self):
  instructions

2. Object: An object is an instance of a class.
-Objects have attributes/properties and method.
-We can create multiple objects from the same class, each with their own attribute values.
Syntax:-
  object1 = className(arguments)

3.Methods :- Methods are functions defined inside a class that describes the behaviors of an object.
-All methods have "self" keyword as their first parameter, which allows them to access and modify the object's attribute.
Syntax:- Class className (self):
  iinstructions

4. Self keyword :-Self refers to the current object or current instance of the class.
-It enables each object to maintain it's own attribute values.
-Without self keyword, python would not know which object's data to use in methods or constructor.

5. Constructor:-A constructor is a special method which is called automatically when an object is created from a class.
-It is used to initialize the object's attribute or set initial state.
__init__ is the constructor in python, which is defined inside the class.
Syntax:-
  class className:
  constructor(self, parameters):
  self.variable = parameter

Types of Constructor:-
a> Default Constructor:- It takes no arguments except ‘self’ keyword.
b> Parameterized Constructor:- It takes one or more parameters including self.
-It initializes objects attribute with user value.

Polymorphism:
-It means "many forms". It allows a function or method with the same name to perform differently depending on the type of object.
e.g: class Dog:
  def speak(self):
  print("Woof")
class Cat:
  def speak(self):
  print("Meow")
dog1 = Dog()
dog1.speak()
cat1 = Cat()
cat1.speak()

Encapsulation:- Encapsulation is an OOP principle that involves binding data and methods within a class and restricting direct access to some of the class's components.
-It improves security, and maintainability of code by controlling how data is accessed and updated.
-We can achieve encapsulation by converting public variables into private variable by using double underscore(__) before the variable name.
-It Keeps our code organized and secure by ensuring that objects protect their own data and expression only what is necessary.
eg: class Bank:
  def __init__(self, account , balance):
  self.__balance = balance
  self.account = account
  def desposit(self,amount):
  if amount > 0:
  self.__balance += amount
  else:
  print("Invalid amount")
  def withdraw(self,amount):
  if amount > 0 and amount <= self.__balance:
  self.__balance -= amount
  else:
  print("Invalid amount")
  def get_balance(self):
  return self.__balance
account1 = Bank("123456", 1000)
print(account1.get_balance())
account1.desposit(100)
print(account1.get_balance())
account1.withdraw(500)
print(account1.get_balance())

Abstraction: Abstraction means hiding the internal details and showing only the necessary features.
-In Python it can be implemented by using ABC(Abstract Base Classes) and the @abstract methods.
Syntax:-
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
class ClassName(ABC):
 	@abstractmethod
 	def methodName(self):
 		pass
 
-Subclass must implement the abstract method.

Inheritance: It allows a class to inherit methods and properties from another class.
-The class from where we inherit is called parent class, and the class which inherits is called child class.
-Inheritance promotes code reusability.
Syntax:-
class ParentClass:
 	def methodName(self):
 		//instruction
 
class ChildClass(ParentClass):
 	//instructions
-Inheritance extends parent class functionality.

Error:-
Errors in programming referred to bugs or problems within a program that prevents it from functioning.
-There are 4 types of errors in Python:-
 	1)Syntax Error :- Mistakes in code structure like missing semicolon, spelling mistake for keywords, which detected before execution.
 	2)Runtime Error :- Occurs while code runs, such as ZeroDivisionError.
 	3)Logical Error :- Code runs but produces unexpected results due to wrong logic.
 	4)Indentation Error :- Occurs due to incorrectly indented code blocks.
-Errors in software or program is called bugs, and fixing errors or bugs is called debugging.
-Some common errors are:- TypeError, ZeroDivisionError, ImportError, etc.

Exception: An exception is a special type error that interrupts normal flow of program.
-When an error occurs, Python creates an exception object that contains details about the error or problem, and then throws it to stop the execution.
-Python includes many built-in exception classes, all are derived from Exception.

Exception Handling: Exception handling separates error detection from business logic using try-except-finally blocks.
 	try block:- We write codes in try block which may occurs exception or error.
 	except block:- Handles exceptions, also we can specify specific type for response.
 	finally block:- It runs always, whether there is exception or not.
Syntax:-
try:
 	//instruction
except:
 	//instruction
finally:
 	//instruction
-We can specify specific and multiple exception block within a program, which makes programs safer and easier to debug, and allows custom responses to differ error types.

##NumPy: NumPy is a powerful python library for numerical computing which is known for its fast, versatile and advanced mathematical capabilities
-NumPy is an open-source library developed to perform numerical operations on large, multidimensional array and matrices
-NumPy stand for numerical Python
-It supports various mathematical algebraic and statistical functions making it easy for data science and machine learning
-NumPy arrays are known as nD arrays representing homogenous collection of data delivering up to 50 times faster than python lists.

# Difference between array and python list
NumPy arrays called homogeneous type of data while list calls heterogeneous type of data.
NumPy arrays is much faster for large data-set due to its memory layout but lists are slower because of their dynamic structure.
NumPy arrays supports advanced mathematical which is compare with list.
NumPy arrays consume less memory as they are stored in contiguous memory blocks.
lists are mutable but NumPy arrays are immutable.
NumPy arrays are used for numeric computation while python lists are used for general purpose only.

-itemSize Operator:  It gives the size in bytes of each element in a NumPy array determined by its datatype.
-dtype Operator: It returns the datatype of a NumPy array like int32 float64 etc.
-size Operator: It returns the total numbers of elements in the array
-shape Operator: It returns a tuple indicating the size of the array along each dimension. ex: (2,3)

##Array Creation and basic manipulation Methods
NumPy offers functions to create different different types of arrays conveniently, the methods are:
1.array- It creates arrays from Python list
2.zeros()- It creates an array filled with zeros only
3.ones()- It creates an array filled with ones only
4.empty()- It Creates an uninitialized array
5.arrange()- It creates an array with regularly Spaced Values. It accepts three arguments starting range, ending range and steps respectively.
6.reshape()- It reshape an array without changing the original data. It accepts two arguments row and column respectively.
7.linspace()- It creates an array within a certain range where each elements spaced linearly from each other. It accepts three arguments starting point, ending point and numbers of elements
8.min and max - It returns minimum value and maximum value respectively
9.sum()- It returns the sum of all the numbers in an array, also we can sum the values row wise and column wise by providing axis value where 0 means column and 1 means row
10.sqrt()- it is used to find the square root of a NumPy array
11.dot()- It is used to find the matrix product of an NumPy array
12.power()- It is used to find the power of an NumPy array.
13.ravel()-It is used to flatten a n-dimensional array to 1-D array


##Indexing in NumPy array:-
NumPy array supports indexing which helps us to access data.
We can access a single element by using its index value.
NumPy array also supports negative indexing same as python list.
For 2-D array use comma separated indices [[1,2],[3,4]] first value is row and second value is column.

##Slicing:
We can extract a range of elements from an array by using slicing, The multidimensional array: arr[0:2, 1:5] selects a block of row and columns

Iteration:
We can iterate over an array with a simple for loop.
Syntax : for i in array, It iterates over an 1-D arrays
for multidimensional use nested loop
Syntax : for r in array, Here r iterates over each row

Stacking:
We can combine arrays which called stacking, There are two ways to stack arrays:
1.Vertically: To stack array vertically we have to use 'V-Stack' method
2.Horizontally: To stack array horizontally or side by side we have use 'h-stack' method


##Boolean Indexing: We can store an array as Boolean by using conditions. ex: arr>5, arr<5 etc. This technique is great for filtering data and creating new array with selected value.

-Pandas is widely used library for data manipulation and analysis specially in Data science and machine learning
-Pandas is an open-source library that provides flexible and efficient tools to analyze, clean, explore and manipulate dataset in python.
-It offers two types of data structures:
1)	Series- Which means one dimensional
2)	DataFrame - Which is Two dimensional, similar to Excel Sheet or SQL table
-It was created by Wes McKinney in 2008 On Top of NumPy to combine the power of Array competition with higher level Data Structure
-With Pandas we can load data From Various Format like csv, excel, database, JSON etc.

# Use Case of Pandas:-
1.Data Cleaning:
2.Data Transformation
3.Data storage
4.Machine Learning Overflow

##DataFrame
-A Dataframe in pandas is a powerful 2-D, tabular data structure that makes it easy to manipulate and analyze the data.
-To create a dataframe there are several techniques:
1.From a dictionary:
import pandas as pd
data = { 'Name': ['abc','def','ghi']
         'Age' : [12,13,14]
          'City': ['a','b','c']
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
print (df)

# From dictionary
df = pd.DataFrame({'A':[1,2], 'B':[3,4]})

# From list of lists
df = pd.DataFrame([[1,2,3],[4,5,6]], columns=['X','Y','Z'])

# From NumPy array
arr = np.array([[1,2,3],[4,5,6]])
df = pd.DataFrame(arr, columns=['A','B','C'])

# From CSV/Excel
df = pd.read_csv("file.csv")
df = pd.read_excel("file.xlsx")

# From JSON
df = pd.read_json("file.json")

#from List of Touple
td = [(1,'Sumit',21)
       (2,'Sumit',31) ]
df3 = pd.DataFrame(td,columns = ['sn','Name','Age'])

#From list of dictionary
ld = [
{'name' : 'sk', 'Age' :21}
{'name' : 'ab' , 'Age' :25}
]

df4 = pd.DataFrame(ld)
df4

#Reading and Writing on CSV file
-To Read a csv file we need to use read_csv() method and we have to pass file name/path as an argument to the method. 
ex: pd.read_csv("File_name.csv")

-To skip n number of rows while creating a dataframe from a csv file either we can use skiprows attribute or header attribute
ex: df = pd.read_csv("name.csv", skiprows = 5)
df2 = pd.read_csv("name.csv", header=5)

-When we don't have any table heading we can use "header = True". It will automatically generate column names that is index values.
df2 = pd.read_csv("name.csv", header=True)

-We can also provide names for each column by using names attribute.
pd.read_csv('a.csv', header = None , names = ['col1','col2'])

-We can read the limited number of rows from a csv file to create a dataframe by using nrows attribute which excludes header.
pd.read_csv("a.csv", nrows = 5)

-In case of missing cells with data then we can initialize NaN values by using na_values attribute , it accepts number of arguments which will be replaced by NaN values 
pd.read_csv("a.csv", na_values = ['a','b'])
-It is useful for cleaning data files.

-If we want to initialize NaN Values to Some specific Column only , then we can replace by using dictionary format.
pd.read_csv("a.csv", na_values = {'a':1 ,'b':2})

-To create a csv file from a dataframe then we can use 'to_csv' method and we have to pass a name as an argument for the new csv file
df.tp_csv("a.csv")

-However add a new column for the index values we can skip the index values by using index = False
df.tp_csv("a.csv",index = False)

-To create csv file based on some specific columns then we have to mention specify columns name by using columns attribute
df.tp_csv("a.csv", columns = ['f','s'],index = False)

-Also we can skip header while creating file by using "Header = False"
df.tp_csv("a.csv",Header = False)


Read and Write Excel File
-To read an excel file create a dataframe we need to use read_excel() method and have to pass the excel file name as an argument
pd.read_excel("file_name.xlsx")

-To read a specific sheet from an excel file we have to mention the sheet name inside the read method 
pd.read_excel("file_name.xlsx", sheet_name = 'sheet2')

-To replace any cell from a specific column we have to create a function and pass it as an argument for converters
def convert_gender(cell):
    if cell == "female":
        return 'women'
    return cell

def convert_age(cell):
    if cell == 20:
        return 21
    return cell

df2 = pd.DataFrame(data2, converters 
                   = {
    'gender' : convert_gender,
    'age' : convert_age
})

-We can write or create an excel file from a DataFrame by using to_excel() method. We have to pass new file name as an argument.
df.to_excel("file_name.xlsx")

-However it will add an extra column for the index values, we can skip the index values by using index = False 
df.to_excel("file_name.xlsx", index = False)

-We can use sheet_name attribute to provide a name for the sheet during writing 
-We can start our new excel file from a specific row number and also column numbers by using 'startrow' and 'startcol' attributes
df.to_excel("file_name.xlsx", index = False, startrow = 3, startcol = 3)

-We can write two or more DataFrames to a single excel file by using excel writer method and writer keyword
with pd.ExcelWriter('fileNAME.xlsx') as writer

#Handling Missing Data:
-In case of any cells with some missing data or were missing some rows of data then we can handle it by using various method.
-There are methods which we can use to handle missing data such as fillna(), dropna(), replace() etc.  

## Fillna() 
-It is used to replace the NaN values with a specific value in a dataFrame. 
-It will search all the cell in the DataFrame and it will replace all the NaN values with the specific value that we will pass as an argument for fillna() method .
Syntax: df.fillna("newVALUE")

-If we want to replace each column with a different value then we can replace by passing dictionary format as an argument to fillna() method .
Syntax: df.fillna("newvalue")
'col':''vaalue'
'col2': 'value'
})

-If we want to fill NaN cell with its previous cell value the we can do it by using method = ffill argument for fillna() method , where 'ffill' stands for forward fill.
Syntax: df.fillna(method = 'ffill')

-Also we can use method = bfill, which means backward fill. 
-It will fill the NaN cell with next cells value 
Syntax: df.fillna(method = 'bfill')

-Also we can provide axis value to the fillna() method and according to the axis value it will fill the NaN cell , with forward or backward data 
Syntax: df.fillna(method = 'ffill', axis = 'columns')

-We can Set a limit to fill the empty or NaN cell with previous or next values by using limit attribute . 
-It will set a limit for how many cells we want to fill the data from next or previous cell.
Syntax : df.fillna(method='ffill', limit = 2)

-For numeric values we can predict the empty cells value based on previous and next cells value by using interpolate() method , it will predict the value based on interpolation 
Syntax : df.fillna(interpolate())

-There are various arguments that we can pass to interpolate method such as method = linear/nearest/quadratic/cubic/time...etc.
-By default method value is linear 


## Dropna():
-If we want to delete or drop the any row which contains NaN values then we can drop by using dropna() method 
-We can use have parameter to drop rows based on some condition 
Syntax: df.dropna(how='all') It will drop all the rows if all the cells from that row is NaN .

-If we want to drop rows based on some conditions like if a row contains atleast one or two or n numbers of non NaN values then keep the row in that case we can use thresh attribute .
Syntax: df.dropna(thresh = 1)

-We can create a range of date by using date_range() it will create a range of date from start date to end date which we have to pass as an argument and the date format should be mm-dd-yyyy

##Replace() : To replace any value in a DataFrame we can use replace() method. It accepts two parameter : 
1.Search value or Old value
2.New Value

Syntax: df.replace(searchValue, newValue)

If we want to replace any value with nan value we can use numpy as a nan value
Syntax: df.replace(searchValue, np.NaN)

To replace multiple values we can use list format.
syntax: df.replace([val1,val2,val3], newval)

If we want to replace some value for any specific column then we have to use dictionary format  
syntax: df.replace({'col1': seaarchValue,
                     'col2' : searchValue}, newValue})
When we want to replace a specific value then also we can use the dictionary format which called as mapping 
syntax: df.replace({'val': newVal1,
                     'val2' : Newval2})

By using regex we can replace values based on some pattern.
Syntax: df.replace({
                      'col1' : '[A-Za-z]'}, "newVALUE",
                       regex = True)

To replace the list of value with another list of value we have to pass both the list as an argument for replace method, both the list should have same number of element 
Syntax: df.replace([list1],[list2])

##Group By
To group data from a DataFrame We can use Group by method it will filter all the data and create groups with similar data we have to pass an argument to the group by method based on which it will group the similar data.
Syntax : df.groupby('colName')

We can use for loop to iterate over the group
for groupname, group_Data in group
    print(groupname)
    print(group_Data)

To access a specific group DataFrame we can use get_group method and we have to pass a group name as an argument then it will return the entire groups dataFrame 
Syntax : gr.get_group(groupname)

To find out the maximum value of each group we can use max method it will return the maximum value from each column or each group.
Syntax : gr.max()
This process is called as split-apply-combine.

##Mean : It is used to return the average value , 

## Describe : To get all the details or get the analytics in one shot we have to use describe method .
Syntax : gr.describe()

##Concat() :  The concat method is used to concatenate two or more DataFrames along a specified axis either rows or columns to produce a new dataframe
Syntax : gr.concat([df1,df2,df3])

If we use ignore_index = True , the resulting dataframe will have a new integer index discarding the original indices  by ignoring the default indexing as false 
Syntax : gr.concat([df1,df2,df3], ignore_index = true)

By using Keys attribute we can add a separate index for each Data Frame during Concatenation. It is useful when combining objects with overlapping objects 
By using this key value we can select or retype a single set of data from the cancatenated Dataframe 

We can use the axis attribute to concatenate the dataframe to row wise or column wise , by default axis value is Zero which concatenates row wise or column value 

To create an order for each dtaframe we can use index attribute provide index value
When we will concatenate data frames the s=data will be concatenate based on the index value  

We can also concatenate a datframe by data value 
s = pd.series(['a', 'b', 'c', 'd'])
df = pd.concat([df1,s], axis =1)


##Merged DataFrame 
-When we want to merge two or more dataframe into a single dataframe we have to use merge() method and we have to pass the common column name based on which we want to merge the dataframe 
Syntax : pd.merge('df1', 'df2', on = 'common_col_name')

-If the common column values are not same then it will merge only common values 
-merge() method merges the data based on the intersection theory which is also called as inner join  

-If we want to merge the dataframe either left or right we have to use an attribute called 'how' and we have to pass the value either left join or right join 
Syntax : pd.merge(temp, wind on = "city", how = 'left')

-To merge all the data from both the dataframe we have to use how = 'outer' it is called outer join.’
Syntax : pd.merge(temp, wind on = "city", how = outer)

-To indicate the non-common data are from which dataframe we have to use indicator attribute

##Pivot 
-It allows us to reshape the DataFrame, and it is used to reshape data based on column value.
-It requires unique index and column combinations.
-Pivot() method fails if there are duplicate combinations of index and columns.
Syntax:- df.pivot(index='ColName', columns = 'ColName', Values = 'ColName')
Ex:- df.pivot(index='Employee', columns = 'Year', Values = 'Salary')

"index = Employee" means rows are employee
columns = Year" means each year becomes a new columns
"Value = Salary" means sale values are salary

-pivot_table() :- It is used to summarize and aggregate the data inside dataframe
-It is similar to pivot method but it can handle duplicate value 
-It allows aggregation using "aggfunc", default value is mean

pivot_table_df = df.pivot_table(
     index = 'Employee',
     columns = 'Year',
     values = 'salary',
     aggfunc = 'sum',
     fill_value = 0
     margins = True
     margin_name = 'Total'
)

Here aggfunc= sum means calculate the sum of salary
fill_value = 0 replaces missing value with zero
margins= True adds all rows or columns
margins_name = Total , name of the column of the total value

melt():- It is used to reshape data from a wide format to long format , it is the reverse of pivot method. 
-It onwards multiple columns into two key columns one is for variable names and the other one is for values 

melt_df = pd.melt(
    df,
    id_vars=['Employee', 'Department'],   
    value_vars=['Salary', 'Bonus'],       
    var_name='New Col',                  
    value_name='Amount'                   
)
Here id_vars defines columns that stay as identifiers 
value_varse defines columns that get unpivoted into rows that name defines a name to value column 

##rename():-
-It is used to rename a column name in a dataframe 
Syntax : df.rename(columns = {'oldName':'newName'},
inplace =True )

columns attributes maps all column names to new column names 
ex:- df.rename(columns={'Employee':'Emp Name'}, inplace=True)

##drop_duplicates():-
-It is used to drop or delete duplicate rows from a dataframe 
Syntax:- df3.drop_duplicates(inplace = True)

##apply():-
-It is used to apply a custom function to a dataframe or a series, it is also used to data transformation, cleaning and feature engineering  

Syntax: df.apply(function)
the function is used to apply to each column or row.
df['New'] = df['Year'].apply(lambda x : x+1)


##sort_values():-
It is used to sort a dataframe or series by one or more columns or index values 
It is one of the most common operations when cleaning or analyzing the data 
Syntax: df.sort_values('by , ascending = True, inplace = True)

by means column names or index values 
ascending = True means , ascending order and False means descending order , by default ascending = true
